# 	

**Running title: Population genomics of *Coccinella septempunctata* across its global range**  
BIOL 624 Fall 2025, Scott Monahan, Elijah Vogler, Kailyn Kiburi, Alexandra McElwee-Adame, Trevor Mugoya, Raya Esplin-Stout, John Obrycki, Alois Honek, Jiri Skuhrovec, Michael Knapp, Arun Sethuraman

#### **Introduction**

	Invasive species are considered one of the most destructive consequences of the anthropocenehuman globalization and transportation. Both inadvertent or intentional Biologicatranslocations areltranslocations invasions are a pervasive drivers of ecological change, affecting ecosystems across taxonomic groups and biomes. Invasive plants such as salt cedars (*Tamarix* species) have altered hydrology and soil chemistry in North American riparian systems (Shafroth et al. 2005), while the introduction of the brown tree snake (*Bioga irregularis* irreularis) on Guam stands as a classic example of an invasive species causing catastrophic declines in native birds species (Fritts & Rodda, 1998). Aquatic invaders such as zZebra mMussels (*Dreissena polymorpha*) have similarly alteredreconstructed community dynamics and nutrient cycling across freshwater systems where they have been introduced (Strayer, 2009). These diverse episodes illustrate a general pattern: once established, invasive species can spread rapidly, exploiting unfilled ecological niches, and outcompeteing native taxa.  
In response to invasive species,  biological control programs have historically introduced predatory insects, parasitoids, and pathogens have been introduced into new regions as biological controls. While these efforts may offer effective suppression, they also risk impactingincluding nontargeted species, competitive displacingement of native species, and shiftings in community structure (Obrycki et al. 2000; Simberloff & Stiling 1996). For coccinellid beetles in particular, interactions between native and non-native species can strongly influence local biodiversity and the overall success of biological control programs (Li et al. 2021). Long-term monitoring has proven the introduction of coccinellids can contribute to the decline of native species across North America, raising questions about the ecological costs of classical biological controls (Harmon et al. 2007). This is hardly a stand alone event, as the use of biological controls has often gone awry. For example, one of the most well known cases is the Indian mongoose (*Herpestes auropunctatus*) which was used as a biological control for rats and venomous snakes, but is now invasive around the world (Berentsen *et al*. 2018, Louppe *et al.* 2020\). Another classic example of a biological control going wrong is the introduction of the cane toad (*Rhinella marina*) to control agricultural pest beetles of sugarcane. Ironically the toads were ineffective predators of the target pest and after introduction wrecked havoc on the ecosystem by poisoning native predators and outcompeting native amphibians (Rollins *et al.* 2016\).  
The integration of genomics and ecological modeling has become increasingly central to understanding invasive species dynamics. Genomic tools can reveal population structure, founder effects, and signatures of human-mediated introductions, helping to reconstruct invasion histories and identify adaptive responses (Kajita et al. 2012; Crowley 2021). Beyond historical reconstruction, the shift toward population genomics allows for a more predictive understanding of biological control. As noted by Sethuraman & Obrycki (2020, 2024), integrating genomic data with demographic modeling is essential for evaluating the long-term sustainability of predator-prey interactions and ensuring that introduced agents remain effective under shifting environmental conditions. Population genetic markers such as microsatellites microsatillites, enable high-resolution tracking of dispersal and gene flow (Kim et al. 2012), while spatial models can forecast spread, evaluate establishment likelihood, and guide management scenarios. These integrative approaches provide a powerful framework for evaluating the ecological consequences of biological control introductions and for developing informed strategies for monitoring and mitigation.  
Within this broader context, the seven-spotted lady beetle *Coccinella septempunctata* serves as an influential model for studying invasive dynamics, biological control, and predator-predator interactions. Introducedtion into North America for aphid suppression, *C. septempunctata* rapidly expanded its distribution, with early records documenting recolonization patterns and ecological traits (Angalet et al.1979; Schaefer et al. 1987). Its spread across the continent led to the morphological and demographic changes during establishment (Evans, 2000)( and generated interactions with native coccinellids that continue to shape community dynamics (Obrycki et al. 1998; Li et al. 2021). With an annotated genome now available (Crowley, 2021), *C. septempunctata* offers an exceptional system of integrating ecological data, genetic tools, modeling approaches to better understand invasive predator establishment and its broader ecological impacts.   
	Invasive species are considered one of the most destructive consequences of the anthropocene, driven largely by human globalization and transportation (Fridley et al. 2007\) human globalization and transportation. When an organism is transported, either whether inadvertently or intentionally, to novel environments, they encounter new ecological conditions that may include abundant resources and release from their native competitors. Under these conditions, certain species become invasive as they establish rapidly,  o a new location, it faces a new suite of challenges and opportunities. Sometimes an organism is presented with plentiful resources and freedom from native constraints, and it becomes invasive. Invasive species spread quickly and displace native populations through competition, predation or ecosystem modification (Friedley et al. 2007; Simberloff & Stiling 1996.). Across the world, Several invasive species have become emblematic of these impacts including certain displaced species have become infamous: Cane toads (*Rhinella marina)* , Zebra mussels *(Dreissena polymorpha)*, Lionfish *(Pterois* spp.). Additionally invasive grass species such as , *PPhragmites australis*, and  Burmese pythons *(Python bivittatus)*, illustrates how introduction can result in widespread and lasting ecological change. ,   
*Coccinella septempunctata* is a common predatory lady beetle whose native range extends from Europe through Russia to Japan and as far south as India, the Middle East, and North Africa (Nikitsky, N. B.). This species typically has 2 generations per year and spends most of the winter under leaf and bark litter in forested areas. In the spring and summer time, the species emerges to eat aphids and other agricultural pests, making it a very popular method of crop pest control (Nikitsky, N. B.). Agricultural groups have attempted deliberate introductions of *C. septempunctata* in North America as far back as 1956 and North American introductions have been tracked by the USDA dating back to a series of failed attempts in California in the mid-1950s.  However, , but the first collection of C. septempunctata in North America did not occur until 1973 (Angalet et al. 1979, G. W; ) (Schaefer et al. 1987, P. W.). Since these initial introductionsn, the population of *C. septempunctata* these insects hasvehas greatly increased in number andnumber exploded and they can be found across the United States (Kajita et al 2012), raising questions regarding impacts on native species, (ecological plasticity?)reasons behind their successful establishment in a wide range of habitats, and whether population increases are a result of multiple successful agricultural introductions or simply population expansion of the first established population.  
	Introductions of *C. septempunctata* to North America have been tracked by the USDA dating back to a series of failed attempts in California in the mid-1950s. As the population of *C. septempunctata* has grown following successful introductions (Schaefer, P. W.), a variety of questions have been raised. Has thisDid the introduction of a potentially invasive species harm any native species? Are the introductions working, or is the population growth due to expansion from the first established population? And why is *C. septempunctata* so successful in novelso many new environments? The question of whether or not *C. septempunctata* has consequences on other *Coccinella* species has proven difficult to answer (e.g. Evans 2017). Simberloff and Stiling (1996) approached this question through a highly critical analysis of biological control processes as a whole and found little evidence for detrimental effects of deliberate releases despite their skepticism surrounding the lack of biological/ecological impacts of such introductions. SkepticalWhile skeptical that any introduction of a new species can be done safely and without biological/ecological consequences, they concede that they found little evidence of detrimental effects due to these releases., and that Furthermore, introductions were effectively havingthey are having the desired effect of reducing  the populations of Russian wheat aphids which that are common agricultural pestswould otherwise harm agricultural crops (Simberloff & Stiling 1996, D.). In 1998, Obrycki et al. also found no substantial differences in population size or growth rates of a native *Coccinella* species after successful introduction of *C. septempunctata* (Obrycki, J. J., 1998). However, in a follow-up study in 2000, Obrycki found reductions in population density of *Adalia bipunctata* and *Coccinella transversoguttata* after introduction of *C. septempunctata* in South Dakota (Obrycki, J. J., 2000). Following the publication, follow-up Other studies have confirmed these population declines of native species (Li, H.), (Harmon, J. P.).   
	While invasive conccinelids often thrive through high connectivity and admixture, native species may lack these adaptive advantages. Recent genomic analysis of the native *Hippodamia convergens* revealed unexpected population fluctuations and remarkably low gene flow across its range (Sethuraman et al. 2024). This compltete contrast suggests that their relative isolation compared to the highly mobile and interconnected populations of invasive species such as the *C. septempunctata.*  
 Both the The degree of and reasons for their success across diverse habitats outside their native range remainsquestion of how successful *C. septempunctata* introductions have been is also unclear. have been also has no single clear answer. Obviously, the CThough current populations of *C. septempunctata* are largeis large and robust (Gordon, & Vandenberg), which thebut the cause appears to be a factor of both mix between population expansionspread and multiple successful human introductions (Kajita et al. 2012, Y). This leads naturally to questions about why how *C. septempunctata* is so successful in so many different diverse regions. Additionally, Evidence supports Evans proposes that *C. septempunctata* is a particularly good generalist when compared to other *Coccinella* species, as determined by variance in body size and development rates as a proxy for success in resource competitioncompeting for resources (Evans, E. W. 2000\) (NOTE: sentence is not clear whether there is variance between species or within *C. septempunctata*.). The success of *C. septempunctata in novel environments* mirrors broader patterns observed in other invasive lady beetles (Arnold et al 2022). For instance, global expansion of the *Harmonia axyridis* have been linked to specific genomic and phenotypic variations that facilitates rapid adaptation (Li et al. 2023). Furthermore, recent comparative work suggests that diverse invasive lady beetle species may utilize shared patterns of genomic variation to navigate the challenges of rapid range expansion(Jones et al. 2024). Interestingly, while invasive *coccinellids* often exhibit high connectivity, native generalist predators in the same regions (such as *Hippodamia convergens*) can show unexpected population fluctuations and significantly less gene flow (Sethuraman et al. 2024), highlighting a potential divergence in the colonization strategies of native versus invasive taxa.   
	 Previous studies on the population genetic structure related to distribution of *C. septempunctata* have produced measures consistent with historical and ecological accounts, but their applications are hindered due to the lack of access to a high quality reference genome, nuclear DNA, or sufficient data quality (This sentence is a bit confusing).  Consistent with the expected relationship between genetic diversity and serial re-introductions, analysis of mtDNA revealed data showed it wasKajita et al. found using mitochondrial DNA that *C. septempunctata* populations closer to introduction sites had a greater heterozygosity than further populationsthose further away using mitochondrial DNA (Kajita et al. 2012, Y). Although this result suggests strong population structure, studies using nuclear DNA have indicated strong admixture between a large number of distinct subpopulations (Summerhays 2019). High degrees of admixture between temporarily isolated populations agrees with the migratory nature of *C. septempunctata*, but conflicts with prior findings of serial founder effects. Another previous investigation into the  FST  and ppopulation structure and FST of *C. septempunctata* using ADMIXTURE found 20 subpopulations with a high degree of admixture between geographically distant subpopulations (Summerhays 2019). However, tThis study suffered from quality filtering issues after guided variant calling possibly due to a low quality reference genome, thus the results should be treated with caution. After filtering, only 15,316 loci remained, 20 times fewer than another species that was analyzed in parallel. The recent availability of high-quality, chromosome-level assemblies for related species, such as the *Hippodamia convergens* (Ang et al. 2024), has set a new standard for the field, enabling researchers to detect fine-scale signatures of selection and admixture that were previously masked. By utilizing the annotated genome provided by Crowley (2021), this study overcomes the data quality issues that hindered previous investigations. Since this research was published in 2019, a new *Coccinella septempunctata* reference genome has been published, which was utilized for this study (Crowley 2021, L).   
		  
In this study, we used a whole genome resequencing data from individuals sampled across China, Eastern Europe, Western Europe, and the United States to characterize global population structure in *Coccinella septempunctata.* UThis approach follows recent efforts to assess genome-wide adaptations associated with range expansion in other invasive insects, such as the pink stem borer (Li et al. 2024), providing a comparative baseline for how these predators adapt to novel environments across different continents. Using a high quality referencerefrence genome, we tested whetherwether introduced U.S populations exhibit reduced genetic diversity and signatures of demographic bottlenecks, and evaluated evidence for admixture among regional lineages. …

A better understanding of population structure wouldcould help us determine which deliberate releases were most successful, and how the subpopulations have spread thereafter. since those releases. Furthermore, confirming the high admixture rates between these populations,, regardless of geographic region, could point to frequent, small undocumented releases of *C. septempunctata* as an insecticide-free pest control method by individual farmers. Many small agricultural product stores sell a variety of caught and captive-bred lady beetles online explicitly for this purpose, and few appear to document the species or source of the insects that they are providing. ( NOTE:  Relatively few companies sell C7 in the UK and maybe Europe – I could not find any North American companies that sell C7)

#### **Methods**

Short-read IlluminaⓇ sequence quality control and preprocessing.

We downloaded the *C. septempunctata* reference assembly, GCA907165205.1, from the NCBI RefSeq database and subsequently indexed it with *bwa-mem2* and *samtools faidx* [(Danecek et al., 2021; Vasimuddin et al., 2019\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Q2jKXw). Subsequently, we trimmed 88 whole genome *C. septempunctata* sequences for sequencing adapters and low quality bases with *fastp* (ver.0.20.1) using default parameters [(Chen et al., 2018\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?VebrU0). 

The reference indices and trimmed paired-end whole genome fastq files were each passed as input to *bwa-mem2 mem* (ver.2.2.1) command to perform reference-based mapping to the 9 largest chromosomes in addition to the X chromosome. Unplaced genomic scaffolds were removed from the reference genome file prior to read mapping and thus no reads were aligned to them. The resulting Binary Alignment Map (BAM) files were then passed to samtools –sort and samtools –index commands to coordinate sort and index each sample BAM file respectively.  
   
**Variant Calling**   
The coordinate sorted BAM files and their associated indices in addition to the reference genome and its indices were passed into Genome Analysis Toolkit (GATK) (ver.4.6.2.0) *HaplotypeCaller* to generate per chromosome gVCF variant files per sample. The SNPs for each sample contained in these chromosome-level gVCF files were then merged with *GATK MergeVcfs* command to create sample-level gVCF files. Sample-level gVCF files for all samples were passed as input into GATK’s *GenomicsDBImport* command to create chromosome-level genomic data objects to store variant information prior to joint genotyping. The GATK genomic data objects in conjunction with the *C. septempunctata* reference genome sequence were used as inputs to GATK’s *GenotypeGVCFs* command to jointly genotype SNPs per chromosome to generate chromosome-level VCF files which were all subsequently merged into a single VCF with *MergeVcfs*. The raw VCF file was subsequently filtered with GATK’s *VariantFiltration* command with custom filtering expressions following GATK best practices to create a final VCF file [(Poplin et al., 2018\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?KKwaiP). 

Reference genome indexing, sequence read quality control and reference-based alignment was carried out with the snakemake workflow *snpArcher* [(Mirchandani et al., 2024\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?V5hSEs). Variant calling was performed on a separate snakemake workflow available on GitHub at [https://github.com/ChabbyTMD/GATK\_VARIANT\_CALL](https://github.com/ChabbyTMD/GATK_VARIANT_CALL)  
**Section on basic quality filtration and LD Pruning? – specifying number of starting SNPs, number of SNPs after LD pruning, and maybe give this pruned and filtered dataset a name so we can refer to it throughout the rest of the paper to avoid repetition.**  
 I.e., Our final vcf contained \_\_\_x\_\_\_ SNPs, which was subsequently quality filtered for Minor Allele Frequency(--maf)=0.05, Max Missingness(--max-missing)=0.7, Minimum Quality Score(--minQ)=40, Minimum average read depth(--min-meanDP)=10 and (--maxDP)=30. We used the quality filtered VCF for all analyses. The filtered subset of variants was indexed by *tabix* prior to linkage disequilibrium(LD) pruning[(Danecek et al., 2011; Li, 2011\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?q5ce0f). The filtered VCF file was pruned for LD with PLINK  (ver.1.9.) \[We performed LD pruning with PLINK on the filtered VCF file (--indep-pairwise 50 10 0.25), after which the dataset contained 37,110,919 SNPs. \] The LD pruned and quality filtered VCF was used for all analyses beyond basic diversity statistics (heterozygosity, F, Tajima’s D, Pi?). Linkage disequilibrium pruning was applied to reduce non-independence among loci and to meet the assumptions of downstream multivariate analyses. 

**Genomic Diversity**  
We calculated observed heterozygosity and inbreeding coefficient (F) across all individuals using the quality filtered unpruned variant calling file. Observed homozygosity and Fis (inbreeding coefficient) were calculated for each individual using vcftools (–het function; \_\_\_\_\_\_\_). The –het function outputs observed homozygosity, thus observed heterozygosity was subsequently calculated in RStudio (v. 2025.09.2+418) where heterozygosity \= 1-observed homozygosity. We then calculated mean observed heterozygosity and mean inbreeding coefficient (F) per locality (Fig 2A, Fig 2C) and per region (Fig 2B, Fig 2D).  
Population Structure Analysis: ADMIXTURE  
~~After basic quality filtration, our VCF contained \_\_\_\_\_\_x\_\_\_\_\_\_ SNPs, thus in order to run ADMIXTURE analysis, further filtering and pruning were required. We performed LD pruning with PLINK (--indep-pairwise 50 10 0.25), after which the dataset contained 37,110,919 SNPs~~. Prior to ADMIXTURE analysis, the LD-pruned dataset was thinned further by taking a random subset of approximately 1.1 million SNPs (PLINK: \--thin 0.03) to make the dataset manageable for analysis. The final thinned dataset used for ADMIXTURE analysis contained 367,692 SNPs. We performed ADMIXTURE analysis for K=1 \- K=5 with 10-fold cross-validation (CV) error.  
Individual samples were grouped into four different subpopulations based on the continental location ( China \[CHN\], Eastern Europe \[EEUR\], Western Europe \[WEUR\] and the united states \[USA\]; see Table \_\_) . To quantify genetic differentiation among the geographic populations (AME, EEUR, CHN, and WEUR), pairwise Weir and Cockerham’s Fst coefficients were calculated using VCFtools from the LD-pruned VCF dataset (Weir and Cockerham, 1984\). Initial exploration of genome-wide differentiation was performed in R using the qqman and ggplot2 packages. To address high marker density and improve visual clarity during the plotting process, scatter plots were generated using a representative subsample of 100,000 randomly selected SNPs. For the final comparative analysis, global mean Fst values were derived for each population pair after removing missing values (NAs),  . Data were preprocessed to remove missing values (NAs), and the with means was calculated across all valid loci to provide a summarizey of the overall genetic divergence between the sampled regions.   
Individual samples were grouped into four different subpopulations based on the continental location (refer to table with list of individual samples). Subpopulations included China, Eastern Europe, Western Europe and USA. Pairwise genetic distances with Weir and Cockerham’s Fst were measured between each subpopulation to quantify genetic differentiation between them (Weir and Cockerham, 1984). 

Tajima's D was calculated for each population separately using vcftools (v0.1.16; Danecek et al., 2011\) with the \--TajimaD function applied to non-overlapping 5 kb windows across all chromosomes.  
This approach generated windowed Tajima's D estimates for each chromosome within each population independently, allowing direct comparison of demographic signatures across populations and evolutionary contexts.  
Nucleotide diversity (π)  was calculated using vcftools with the \--window-pi function, employing the same 5 kb non-overlapping windows and population-specific sample subsets. This generated per-window π estimates for each population, enabling quantification of genetic diversity levels within populations and comparison across the invasion gradient.  
The sex chromosome (NC\_058198.1) was included in per-chromosome calculations but excluded from autosomal genome-wide summaries.  
**HWE**  
**![][image2]**  
Calculating Hardy Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE) p-values and visualizing them with a QQ plot provided a structure check for the genomic dataset. Under ideal conditions with randomly mating populations, HWE p-values should follow the expected uniform distribution, producing a QQ plot that aligns closely with the diagonal. However, the pronounced upward deviation observed in our QQ plot indicates that far more loci than expected show significant departures from HWE. This pattern suggests that the deviations are biological rather than technical in origin, reflecting the complex demographic history of *Coccinella septempunctata*, including repeated introductions, founder effects, and substantial admixture among previously isolated lineages. Such widespread HWE deviations are consistent with earlier work on this species, where strong admixture and population structure were detected across North American populations (Summerhays et al., 2019). Thus, the QQ plot not only validates that the dataset captures real underlying structure.

**PCA**  
Prior to PCA plot generation, the *C. septempunctata* VCF file was further filtered for the following parameters; Minor Allele Frequency(--maf)=0.05, Max Missingness(--max-missing)=0.7, Minimum Quality Score(--minQ)=40, Minimum average read depth(--min-meanDP)=10 and (--maxDP)=30. The filtered subset of variants was indexed by *tabix* prior to linkage disequilibrium(LD) pruning[(Danecek et al., 2011; Li, 2011\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?q5ce0f). The filtered VCF file was pruned for LD with PLINK  (ver.1.9.) followed by PCA eigenvector and eigenvalue files were generated with default parameters. The PLINK eigenvector file was modified to reflect the region of origin for each sample using a custom bash script [(Purcell et al., 2007\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?NNTV9s). PCA clusters were subsequently visualized with *ggplot* in *R* (ver.4.4.3) [(Wickham et al., 2019\)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YCMJRr)  
**Phylogenetic Tree**  
Using IQTree and nuclear variants we constructed our genome wide phylogenytree…  
SNP data were obtained from the multi-sample VCF, which had been previously filtered for quality and LD pruning. The VCF was indexed and filtered to retain only biallelic SNPs using bcftools (bcftools view \-m2 \-M2 \-v snps), and then converted to a multi-sequence SNP alignment with the Python script vcf2phylip (Python 3.11), producing FASTA/PHYLIP alignment files. Maximum-likelihood phylogenetic inference was performed in IQ-TREE v2.x using the SNP-only variable-sites PHYLIP alignment. We specified a GTR+ASC+G4 substitution model and assessed node support with 1,000 ultrafast bootstrap replicates and 1,000 SH-aLRT tests. The bootstrap consensus tree (majority-rule) was used for interpretation and was midpoint-rooted for visualization.  
**Selection Statistics**  
We calculated Tajiima’s D across each chromosome and performed outlier tests using OUTFLANK. From this we selecting the top 10 outliers on each chromosome and performed a literature review to determine potential genes under selection  
**Species Distribution Modeling**  
Using occurrence data from XX and climate data from WorldClim?XXX we compiled and ran an SDM using  XXX.  
To understand invasion dynamics of *C. septempuncta* in an ecological context, we created several Species Distribution Models (SDMs), also known as ecological niche models (ENMs). Using a maximum entropy model (MaxEnt: Phillips et al. 2004\) with the in R (package: dismo v1.3-16) (Hijman et al. 2024), we projected global habitat suitability based on both current and future climatic variables. Climate data was obtained for 19 bioclimatic variables with 2.5 arc minute spatial GPS resolution (bio 2.5m) from the WorldClim global climate database (citation). Occurrence data was obtained from GBIF, using only present occurrences with locality coordinates. We ran several different models using present-day climate (\~1970-2000) filtering GBIF data for (1) global occurrences across the Northern Hemisphere (Fig1a \_\_\_), (2) North American occurrences (Fig \_\_\_), and (3) native range occurrences in Asia, Northern Africa and Europe (Fig \_\_\_). These models were run using both We also ran all models based on (1) preserved specimen data (Fig1a \- \_\_\_\_\_\_\_) and (2) observational data (Fig \_\_\_). Preserved specimen data provides us with an occurrence dataset with higher accuracy identification of composed only of confirmed specimens of *C. septempunctata.*  while oObservational data, which (includiengs citizen science reports) but also covers a wider range of occurrences, potentially captures a greater area of habitat suitability, but may include identification errors. introduce identification which introduces identification uncertainty but also potentially captures a greater area of habitat suitability.  
 Prior to model training, we filtered occurrences for removed duplicated localities and coordinate points within 1km2 \- 100km2 of each other (R packages: BiodiversityR, geosphere) to avoid spatial autocorrelation. We then layered all 19 climatic variables prior to initial model training and subsequently removed highly correlated environmental variables prior to final model projection based on Pearson’s correlation coefficient (Pearson’s r) calculations. Correlated variable pairs with Pearson’s correlation coefficient values greater than 0.9 were assessed. From each correlated pair, the variable with the lower contribution to the model was removed.   
We trained each model on the filtered occurrence data and climate data cropped to include only areas of the world included in the occurrence data for that particular model. Distribution projections were made to include the whole world as well as specific regions for higher resolution (Figs \_\_\_\_). We also plotted the model to visualize what variables were the best predictors of the projected distributions for each model and plotted the predicted habitat suitability for each climatic variable to visualize optimal ranges for our species (Fig \_\_\_).   
*Northern Hemisphere preserved specimen data projected globally \-* We downloaded preserved specimen data from the Northern Hemisphere from GBIF (n \= 9,646) and climate variable data for 19 bioclimatic variables from WorldClim. Climate variables were concatenated into a single raster stack and subsequently merged to occurrence data in R. The occurrence dataset was then thinned to remove duplicate localities and datapoints within 100km^2 of one another to avoid spatial autocorrelation, yielding a final dataset of 967 specimens. We ran a maximum entropy (MaxEnt) model in R, training the model on both the occurrence data and the global climate data cropped to the area including the occurrences (i.e., Northern Hemisphere climate). This model was then used to predict habitat suitability globally by projecting the prediction to a climatic map of the world. Following prediction, we plotted the 19 biocliamtic variables (plot(pred)) to assess their individual contributions to the model prediction. We then ran correlation analysis to assess the 19 bioclimatic variables for correlation using Pearson’s correlation coefficient. We compared the resulting list of correlated variable pairs (Pearson’s r \> 0.9) to the original plot of bioclimatic variables to assess which variables would be removed. When comparing each pair, the variable with the lower overall contribution to the model was removed. After removal of correlated variables, we ran the model prediction again and re-plotted the remaining bioclimatic variables to assess their contributions to the model prediction.  
*European observational data projected globally:* We repeated the above process but with citizen observational data from Europe only and projecting that model to the world. 490,442 observations were downloaded from GBIF. This dataset was subsequently thinned for duplicate localities and datapoints within 100km^2 of one another were removed, yielding a dataset of 2,238 observational datapoints used for model training and global prediction.   
*North American observational data projected globally*: We repeated the process used for the Northern Hemisphere specimen data model for citizen observational data from North America (introduced range) alone. We downloaded an observational dataset from GBIF containig 74,538 points from North America. This dataset was subsequently thinned to remove duplicate localities and datapoints within 100km^2 of one another. The model was trained on a final thinned dataset containing 1,765 datapoints and habitat suitability prediction was projected globally.   
Future distribution patterns will be assessed by incorporating the MIROC6 global climate model. MIROC6 provides future climate predictions for atmospheric and oceanic variables like horizontal wind speed, temperature, surface pressure, specific humidity, and cloud water (Ando et al., 2021). The same species occurrence points and filtering methods were used for our future predictions. Similar methods have been used to evaluate potential range expansion for similar taxa (Coleoptera) under the same climate model (Zhang et al., 2025). 

**Demographic History**  
	To estimate the evolutionary history, we estimated several demographics using a coalescent simulator.  
	In order to estimate the parameters to establish the models, four separate vcf files were generated using the whole genome vcf file, each for a separate population (China, eastern Europe, western Europe, United States) using bcftools. Then nucleotide diversity was calculated from each of the population vcf files using vcftools, and effective population size was estimated using the pi statistic and an assumed mutation rate of 10\-8 to match the mutation rate of *Drosophila Melanogaster* (Haag-Liautard et al., 2008).  
	Simulations were run using a MSFS generated using EasySFS (Overcast, 2023.) (Gutenkunst et al., 2009\) and a down sized vcf file using 100,000 SNPs. The coalescent simulator fastsimcoal28 (Excoffier and Foll, 2011\) was used to estimate demographic histories, with random topologies being generated using the CoalMiner pipeline (Citation?). 100 random topologies were made, with each topology being simulated 100,000 times.

To further ensure reproducibility and scalability of downstream analyses, a fully automated workflow was built using Snakemake (Mölder et al., 2021). The Snakemake pipeline integrates all computational steps mentioned above for *Coccinella septempunctata* genomics analyses and organizes output files consistently and in the correct order to ensure that the pipeline stays reproducible. This pipeline includes general population genomics analyses such as variant filtration, linkage disequilibrium pruning, population genetic summary statistics (mean heterozygosity and mean F per locality and per region), population structure analyses (PCA, ADMIXTURE, FST), runs of homozygosity (ROH) and genome-wide homozygosity (FROH). Phylogeny tree plots, plots for Tajima’s D across per chromosome and per population, species distribution models, selection statistics, and demographic histories were also included in this pipeline. The C7 pipeline executes at the per-chromosome and per-individual level. The pipeline produces Manhattan plots for ROH, Froh barplots, and summary tables for genetic diversity standards as well as visual plots to illustrate these statistical analyses. Software dependencies, intermediate files, and parameters are tracked by the workflow enabling immediate producibility. Our C7 pipeline provides an automated population genomic analyses framework for large-scale population genomic analyses.  

**Selection Outlier Detection**  
Given the substantial variation in background FST levels across population comparisons (ranging from 0.002 to 0.055) \[will double-check values soon\], we employed a dual-method approach to detect outliers.  
For the lower-Fst comparisons (mean FST \< 0.02), which would be the comparisons among the invasive populations (EEU vs. WEU, EEU vs. USA, WWE vs. USA), we used OutFLANK analysis (Whitlock & Lotterhos, 2015). OutFLANK assumes most loci evolve neutrally under a common demographic history and identifies statistical outliers from the neutral FST distribution. We applied a q-value threshold of 0.05  
For the higher-Fst comparisons (mean Fst ≥ 0.02), which would be the comparisons between CHI and the invasive populations (CHI vs. USA, CHI vs. EEU, CHI vs. WEU), OutFLANK returned zero outliers due to elevated genome-wide differentiation, likely due to bottleneck events. For these comparisons, we employed percentile-based thresholds, selecting SNPs in the top 0.5% of the empirical Fst distribution as potential selection targets.  
Outlier SNPs were classified into biological categories based on their detection across comparison types: invasion-specific (outliers detected only in CHI vs. invasive population), post-invasion (outliers detected only among invasive populations), found in both contexts (outliers detected in both invasion-specific and post-invasion contexts), and background (SNPs with incomplete comparison data due to missing pairwise results).

**Gene Annotation Pipeline**  
Outlier SNPs were mapped to annotated genes using genomic coordinates from the *Coccinella septempunctata* reference annotation (GCF\_907165205.1). SNPs falling within gene boundaries (start to stop coordinates) were classified as 'genic'. Intergenic SNPs were assigned to the nearest annotated gene.   
Functional annotations were obtained through a dual-source approach. Direct NCBI annotations were retrieved from the NCBI Gene database for *Coccinella septempunctata*. For genes lacking direct NCBI annotations, we employed a two-step BLAST orthology pipeline to leverage both phylogenetic proximity and annotation quality. Coccinella septempunctata proteins were searched against the Harmonia axyridis protein database (23,861 sequences) using BLASTP with minimum 50% identity and E-value ≤ 1e-10. This step maximized sequence identity by using the closest available relative within Coccinellidae. Harmonia axyridis ortholog hits were subsequently searched against Tribolium castaneum proteins (22,272 sequences) with minimum 40% identity and E-value ≤ 1e-10 to access comprehensive functional annotations from this well-characterized model organism. This two-step approach achieved high annotation success rates: 89% Harmonia orthologs and 77% Tribolium orthologs for the candidate gene set, enabling functional characterization of the majority of selection targets.  
Gene functions were manually categorized into biological process groups based on protein descriptions. 

**Selection Candidate Analysis**  
"Both contexts" genes with FST ≥ 0.6 and "invasion-specific genes" with FST ≥ 0.9 were identified as high priority candidates to investigate. \[to be continued…\]

#### **Results**

Tajima’s D  
![][image3]![][image4]![][image5]![][image6]![][image7]  
Comparing Tajima's D across individual populations revealed clear differences among the Coccinella septempunctata lineages. The CHI population displayed a positive Tajima's D value (0.17), while all three invasive populations showed strong negative Tajima's D values: EEU (-0.29), WEU (-0.24), and USA (-0.15). The EEU population exhibited the most pronounced negative Tajima's D (-0.29).  
![][image8]  
Comparing Tajima’s D across individual populations revealed  differences in demographic history among *Coccinella septempunctata* lineages. The CHI population displayed Tajima’s D values centered near zero with numerous positive outliers, consistent with a large, stable, or historically well mixed population retaining substantial intermediate frequency variation. In contrast, the EEUR, US, and WEUR populations showed strongly negative genome wide Tajima’s D values, a pattern characteristic of recent demographic expansion, which aligns with the documented introduction and spread of this species across Europe and North America. The US population, in particular, exhibited the most pronounced negative skew, supporting the hypothesis that invasive populations underwent severe bottlenecks followed by rapid growth after introduction. These population specific signatures echo patterns described in other invasive insects,such as *Harmonia axyridis*,where source populations maintained near equilibrium allele frequencies while introduced populations carried strong negative Tajima’s D associated with invasion driven expansion (Lawson Handley et al., 2011). Thus, this stratified Tajima’s D analysis reinforces a narrative of contrasting demographic trajectories between native or ancestral populations and those shaped by introduction dynamics.

Nucleotide Diversity (π)  
![][image9]  
Population-specific nucleotide diversity analysis revealed a clear demographic hierarchy: CHI (native) \> EEU ≈ WEU \> USA across all autosomes. The CHI population maintained the highest nucleotide diversity (autosomal mean π \= 7.07×10⁻⁴). Among the invasive populations, EEU showed the highest diversity (π \= 6.90×10⁻⁴), followed by WEU (π \= 6.64×10⁻⁴) and USA (π \= 6.22×10⁻⁴), representing 12-14% reductions relative to the native population. Chromosome-specific patterns were consistent across autosomes, with CHI maintaining higher diversity on 8 of 9 autosomes. The USA population showed the most severe diversity reduction. Sex chromosome diversity followed the same population hierarchy but at reduced levels, with CHI showing the highest diversity (π \= 9.07×10⁻⁵) and USA the lowest (π \= 5.87×10⁻⁵).

ROH/FROH  
![][image10]

Genome-wide Runs of Homozygosity (ROH) were calculated across all individuals and per chromosomes using the filtered by chromosome vcf file. The plot above shows several short ROHs clustered near the bottom, suggesting ancient relatedness and low evidence of recent inbreeding among the C7 populations. Some chromosome positions show long ROHs indicative of recent inbreeding or possibly a reflection of a bottleneck event. These long ROHs show moderated to elevated homozygosity ( \> \~1000-2000 kb). There is no specific chromosome clustering which may indicate chromosomes under strong drift or selection. Overall, a by chromosome panel will give a better understanding of ROH genome-wide. 

![][image11]

Froh was calculated per individual summarizing homozygosity for each C7 genome overall. Most individuals lie within 0.2-0.7 indicating moderate homozygosity. These may be due to bottlenecks in these populations. Most notably, one individual has \~0.17, the highest of other C7 individuals. A few other individuals around 0.11-0.14. This indicates that 11-17% of these individual’s genomes are in ROHs, a likely signal of recent inbreeding or strong bottlenecks. 

Figure \_\_:Pairwise Fst between each population:

|  | CHN | EEUR | WEUR | USA |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **CHN** |  |  |  |  |
| **EEUR** | 0.057743 |  |  |  |
| **WEUR** | 0.062363 | 0.014126 |  |  |
| **USA** | 0.060692 | 0.017086 | 0.016026 |  |

Pairwise distance using Weir and Cockerham's Fst were calculated among the four different subpopulations of *C. septempunctata* (Weir and Cockerham, 1984). These values were measurements of genetic differentiation between different populations and the highest values demonstrate high genetic differentiation among these populations and limited gene flow. Generally, these values that were computed here indicate relatively low genetic differentiation. There were higher Fst values between China and all other populations with values greater than 0.05, suggesting that China populations were most genetically dissimilar among the other subpopulations. This interpretation may imply that there was genographic isolation among the China population as well as reduced gene flow. These conclusions are also expected with China as the most geographically distant compared to other populations. There was genetic similarity among the European and USA populations with values less than 0.02, suggesting small genetic differentiation and shared ancestry. There may have been a recent divergence among these populations as well. In conclusion, these results suggest that there is a correlation between population structure and geographic location.      
To quantify genetic differentiation among the geographic populations (AME, EEUR, CHN, and WEUR), pairwise Weir and Cockerham’s Fst coefficients were calculated using VCFtools from the LD-pruned VCF dataset. Initial exploration of genome-wide differentiation was performed in R using the qqman and ggplot2 packages. To address high marker density and improve visual clarity during the plotting process, scatter plots were generated using a representative subsample of 100,000 randomly selected SNPs. The figure below is AME vs CHN manhattan plot attempt using 100k subsampling:  
![][image12]  
For the final comparative analysis, global mean Fst values were derived for each population pair. Data were preprocessed to remove missing values (NAs), and the mean was calculated across all valid loci to provide a summary of the overall genetic divergence between the sampled regions.

|  | USAAME | CHN | EEUR | WEUR |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| USA AME | \- |  |  |  |
| CHN | 0.2369101 | \- |  |  |
| EEUR | 0.03681732 | 0.1889652 | \- |  |
| WEUR | NA Error | NAError | 0.03043984 | \- |

Results: HeterozygosityHeterozygsity and F  
The individuals from the introduced (US) populations had the lowest mean heterozygosity (0.047) and highest mean inbreeding coefficient (0.29) while individuals from the native range (China and Europe) had higher levels of heterozygosity and lower levels of inbreeding (Table \_\_\_). The specific locality with the highest levels of inbreeding and lowest heterozygosity were the Midwest US and Southern US. Samples from Italy and Greece had the lowest levels of inbreeding and highest observed heterozygosity, but sample size from these locations was small (1 and 2 individuals respectively; Table \_\_\_\_\_).  
Results: Selection

Results: Admixture  
Admixture analysis revealed K=1 (1 ancestral population) to be the best explanation of population structure, yielding the lowest cross-validation (CV) error (Fig\_\_\_\_). Admixture barplots revealed very little admixture with nearly all individuals being assigned almost completely to a single ancestral population which is likely a product of low resolution due to using a small subset of SNPs from the dataset.

Results: ENMs: N. Hemisphere specimen data projected globally  
	We removed six bioclimatic variables prior to our final model prediction (Pearson correlation coefficients greater than 0.9: BIO\_1, BIO\_6, BIO\_17, BIO\_5, BIO\_13, BIO\_4). The final model was trained on the remaining 13 bioclimatic variables and projected globally (Fig \_\_\_\_\_). A plot of the bioclimatic variable contribution showed precipitation of the coldest quarter (BIO\_19) to be the greatest contributing variable to the habitat suitability prediction (approx 40% contribution) followed by precipitation of driest month (BIO\_14), mean temperature of coldest quarter (BIO\_11) and mean temperature of warmest quarter (BIO\_10) (Fig \_\_\_\_). The final prediction map projected globally revealed high habitat suitability in Southern South America, Southern Australia, New Zealand, and the southern tip of Africa in addition to the areas of the Northern hemisphere encompassed by the preserved specimen datapoints (Fig \_\_\_\_). The prediction showed low habitat suitability in both very dry and very wet areas, including the Sahara Desert, Saudi Arabia, Antarctica and the Arctic Circle, Northeastern Russia, Northern Australia, Central India, Indonesia, and the majority of  Central and Southern Africa and Madagascar (Fig \_\_\_\_).

Results: ENMs: European Observational Data Projected Globally  
	We removed seven bioclimatic variables prior to our final model prediction (Pearson correlation coefficients greater than 0.9: BIO\_4, BIO\_6, BIO\_14, BIO\_16, BIO\_13, BIO\_11, BIO\_19). The final model was trained on the remaining 12 bioclimatic variables and projected globally (Fig \_\_\_\_\_). A plot of the bioclimatic variable contribution showed annual precipitation (BIO\_12) to be the greatest contributing variable to the habitat suitability prediction (approx 40% contribution) followed by precipitation of driest quarter (BIO\_17), and annual mean temperature (BIO\_1) (Fig \_\_\_\_\_). The final prediction map projected globally revealed high habitat suitability in the Amazon Basin, Central America, the Eastern Coast of Madagascar, Central Africa, Southern and Eastern Australia, Indonesia, Southeast Asia, Southern India and Sri Lanka, and some areas of North America (Alaska, Eastern Canadian Coast, Great Lakes) as well as the area encompassed by the observational datapoints used for model training. Most of the United States and Mexico are considered moderately suitable under this model while Eastern and Central Russia, Central and Eastern Asia, the Sahara Desert, Antarctica and the Arctic Circle were deemed unsuitable (Fig \_\_\_).  
Results: ENMs: N. American Observational Data Projected Globally  
	We removed six bioclimatic variables prior to our final model prediction (Pearson correlation coefficients greater than 0.9: BIO\_11, BIO\_6, BIO\_10, BIO\_7, BIO\_16, BIO\_14). The final model was trained on the remaining 13 bioclimatic variables and projected globally (Fig \_\_\_\_\_). A plot of the bioclimatic variable contribution showed two bioclimatic variables as the primary contributors to the habitat suitability prediction: annual mean temperature (BIO\_1; approx 65% contribution) followed by temperature seasonality (BIO\_4; approx 25% contribution). The final prediction map projected the widest range of habitat suitability with high habitat suitability across Asia (except central China), Europe and North Africa, Australia and New Zealand, southern South America, and the entire United States (Fig\_\_\_\_). Habitat suitability was deemed low in Central Africa, the Amazon Basin, Indonesia, and the Arctic Circle and Antarctica (Fig\_\_\_).  
**Results Phylogenetic Tree**  
![][image13]  
GO Enrichment of Selection Candidate Genes  
![][image14]![][image15]  
\[descriptions coming soon\!\]

**Discussion**  
**Phylogeny**  
Our phylogenetic tree expresses consistent gene flow between geographic regions, with very little grouping outside of immediate populations that were sampled. First, this aligns with the original introduction of this species within North America as biocontrol(SOURCE). These original introductions were sourced from a variety of populations across Europe, which can be seen in the phylogenetic relationships and ADMIXTURE performed in this study. Even still, the degree of mixing between populations suggests recent radiations, multiple secondary introductions and potential back-colonizationpotentially back-colonization.  
**Selection Statistics**  
Our genome-wide outlier analysis identified a number of pathways potentially under selection amonc C. septempunnctata populations. Three major themes emerged. First, several SNPs mapped to transcription factors, chromatin-associated proteins, and post-transcriptional regulators. These all play a critical role in gene expression and epigenetic control. Gene expression is often one of the first adaptations an organism can make in response to environmental changes, therefore it is not surprising that a species with such a broad range is showing selection on genes associated with expression and epigenetics. Our second theme involved a number of SNPs involved with cytoskeletal function and developmental proteins including dynein chain, spindle/centrosome components, and myosin. Cytoskeletal performance is highly sensitive to environmental conditions due to risks associated with protein denaturization.   
Third, several outliers mapped to genes associated with ion homeostasis, metabolic regulation, and cellular stress response.  
All of these patterns indicate that selection within *C. septempunctata* involves changes to regulatory, physiological, and structural components, highlighting an adaptive response to a range of environments.  
**Species Distribution Model**s (ENMs)  
Discussion: Europe (observational), N. America (observational), N. Hemisphere (specimen):  
        	Each projection revealed a different distribution of suitable habitat. The prediction based on the preserved specimen data was much more conservative than either of the predictions based on observational data since observational data covered a wider, more diverse range of habitat with a greater density of datapoints. The preserved specimen data from the northern hemisphere contained a greater number of datapoints from Europe than from Asia and North America, thus this prediction aligns with the predicted distribution using observational data from Europe better than that using observational data from North America. Assessing predicted suitable habitat outside the current range of this species into the Southern Hemisphere, all three predictions show high levels of habitat suitability in southern Australia, New Zealand, southern Africa, and southern South America, suggesting if *C. septemunctata* were to be introduced to these areas, they could establish successfully. This could be especially concerning for island habitats sensitive to invasive species such as New Zealand. When considering the plots of the highest contributing bioclimatic variables, we see similar optimal temperature predictions across all three predictions. According to these results, the optimal predicted temperature based on current distribution is around 20 degrees C (68 degrees F)  
All three predictions have different bioclimatic variables contributing to the greatest percentage, which suggests that there are differences in habitat preference across different areas of this species’ range. When comparing the observational data projections from Europe versus North America, we observe a starkly different distribution of predicted highly suitable habitat, which may be suggestive of local adaptation in the introduced range. The biggest difference appears to be in terms of precipitation where plots of the bioclimatic variable curves show a tolerance/preference for a higher level of precipitation in the Europe prediction than the North American prediction, which is reflected in the suitability map: Europe prediction shows high suitability in the amazon basin and Indonesia while North American Prediction shows very low suitability in these areas. In conjuncture with results from our selection analyses, there appears to be evidence for selection in this species, but more work is required to pinpoint the exact drivers of selection and the potential relative contribution of environmental variables.

Tajima’s D and Nucleotide Diversity (π)

The positive Tajima's D value in the CHI population (0.17) is consistent with a large, stable population maintaining balanced allele frequencies typical of populations at equilibrium, as expected for the native population that has not experienced recent population disturbances or bottlenecks (Tajima, 1989). In contract, the negative Tajima's D values in all invasive populations are characteristic of recent population expansion following bottlenecks, which aligns with the documented introduction history of *Coccinella septempunctata* across Europe and North America (Angalet et al., 1979; Schaefer et al., 1987).  
The magnitude of negative Tajima's D values suggests that the invasive populations experienced founder effects during establishment, followed by rapid population growth and range expansion. The particularly strong negative value in the EEU population (-0.29) suggests it may have experienced the most severe bottleneck or most recent expansion event. These population-specific signatures echo patterns described in other invasive insects, such as Harmonia axyridis, where introduced populations carried strong negative Tajima's D values associated with invasion-driven expansion (Lawson Handley et al., 2011).  
The nucleotide diversity patterns strongly support the demographic interpretation suggested by Tajima's D analysis, with the progressive diversity reduction from CHI → EEU → WEU → USA paralleling the increasingly negative Tajima's D values. The observed nucleotide diversity levels (π \= 6.22×10⁻⁴ to 7.07×10⁻⁴) fall within the typical range reported for insect populations. Similar π values have been documented in other Coleoptera, including Tribolium castaneum populations (π \~ 10⁻³; Wang et al., 2007), invasive Agrilus planipennis populations (π \~ 10⁻⁴; Bai et al., 2011), and Leptinotarsa decemlineata (π \~ 10⁻⁴; Schoville et al., 2018).This concordance between independent measures of demographic history provides robust genomic evidence for the founder effects that shaped invasive C. septempunctata populations during their global spread (Estoup et al., 2016).  
The 12-14% reduction in nucleotide diversity between native (CHI) and invasive populations is consistent with moderate bottleneck effects documented in other successful invasions. For comparison, invasive Harmonia axyridis populations showed 15-25% diversity reductions relative to native populations (Lombaert et al., 2010). The relatively modest diversity loss in C. septempunctata invasive populations suggests either large founding sizes or rapid demographic expansion following establishment, consistent with the species' documented invasion success (Gordon, 1985).

![][image26]  
![][image27]  
**Supplemental Figure A:** Maximum entropy species distribution model trained using 13 non-correlated WorldClim variables (6 climatic variables where Pearson’s correlation was \>0.9 were removed) and 7321 preserved specimens of *C*. *septempunctata* from their native range (Europe and Asia) projected globally. To avoid auto-correlation, specimens locations were filtered so only one specimen every 1km was used. The top predicting variables were the amount of precipitation in the driest month and the annual mean temperature.

![][image28]  
**Supplemental Figure B:** Maximum entropy species distribution model trained using 13 non-correlated WorldClim variables (6 climatic variables where Pearson’s correlation was \>0.9 were removed) and 1209 preserved specimens of *C*. *septempunctata* from their introduced range in North America projected globally. To avoid auto-correlation, specimens locations were filtered so only one specimen every 1km was used. The top predicting variables were the annual mean temperature and the precipitation of the coldest quarter.

![][image29]![][image30]

![][image31]![][image32]

![][image33]![][image34]

![][image35]![][image36]  
![][image37]  
Runs of homozygosity on chromosomes 1-9.   
PLINK v1.90b7.2 64-bit (11 Dec 2023\)  
Options in effect:  
  \--allow-extra-chr  
  \--bfile plink\_input  
  \--homozyg  
  \--homozyg-kb 500  
  \--homozyg-window-het 2  
  \--homozyg-window-missing 2  
  \--homozyg-window-snp 100  
Table of fROH results below location info table  
Búci et al., “Individual Heterozygosity and Fitness in Bottlenecked Populations during Early Colonisation.” A study on spreading populations of a non-native cricket that found correlation between heterozygosity and fitness indicators like body size and population growth rate.
